food_subgroup: Noodle soups — spicy and sour broth-based

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yum Noodle Soup. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent. No specific Tom Yum Noodle Soup code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Tom Yum Noodle Soup. SNOMED CT does not catalogue this dish at the required specificity.

main_ingredients: tom yum broth (lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal, chilli, lime juice, fish sauce)
protein (chicken, pork, seafood, or tofu — choice-based)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Tom Yum Noodle Soup weighing approximately 500 g, comprising broth, noodles, a protein portion, and assorted toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Noodle Soup — composite estimate derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table (2020); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Tom Yum Noodle Soup as a composite dish. Values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for tom yum soup broth, cooked rice noodles, and a mixed protein (chicken/pork/seafood average), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients. Serving-size sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and broth concentration. Values represent a mid-range estimate for a standard bowl with mixed protein and typical toppings.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Tom Yum Noodle Soup composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum Noodle Soup as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated using boiled rice noodles (GI ~40–53, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary glycemic driver, moderated downward by the high water content of the broth, protein content, and acidic lime juice in the tom yum base. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 37.5 g) / 100 ≈ 16.9. Available carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 7.5 g/100 g × 500 g = 37.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and consume the broth sparingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and acidic broth
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 17 per bowl is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should choose a smaller noodle portion, opt for a lean protein such as chicken or tofu, and avoid added sugar-based condiments to better manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from protein and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium load from fish sauce and broth poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit broth consumption, select lean protein options, and be aware that a full bowl may contribute significantly to daily sodium and saturated fat targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Noodle Soup (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวต้มยำ) is a Thai spicy and sour noodle soup dish built on the classic tom yum broth, which is characterised by the aromatic combination of lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal, fresh chilli, lime juice, and fish sauce. The soup is served with a choice of noodle type — typically rice noodles or egg noodles — and a protein such as chicken, pork, mixed seafood, or tofu. Common toppings include mushrooms, bean sprouts, spring onions, and fresh coriander. The broth delivers a bold, complex flavour profile that is simultaneously hot, sour, savoury, and slightly sweet. In Singapore, this dish is widely available through Thai restaurants and food delivery platforms and is popular as a full meal. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 310 kcal, 24 g protein, 9 g fat, and 37.5 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of approximately 2100 mg per serving due to the fish sauce and concentrated broth. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making the carbohydrate load moderate when the full bowl is consumed. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and can be adapted for lower-sodium or lower-carbohydrate diets by reducing broth intake and noodle quantity.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Tom Yum Noodle Soup as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data and USDA SR Legacy reference ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and cross-referenced, but direct measurement data for this specific dish is not available in any approved primary source.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in the broth and ingredient components per USDA SR Legacy data. The dish is inherently variable due to protein choice and topping selection; nutrient values represent a mid-range estimate for a standard bowl with mixed protein. Halal status is marked false because fish sauce (typically not halal-certified) is a standard broth ingredient; halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be assumed. GI category is low (GI = 45, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 37.5) / 100 = 16.9.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from emoji-prefixed field survey menu string to canonical English 'Tom Yum Noodle Soup'.

Added Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names to support Singapore multilingual context.
Estimated per_100g nutrients from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data.
Estimated GI = 45 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry (boiled rice noodles) with full documentation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed negligible in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Set gi_category = 'low' per GI = 45 ≤ 55 threshold rule.
